name: Abhinavagupta
dates_notes: Manuscript colophons and self-references; very well-attested.

lineage_polemical.note: Abhinavagupta's mature Trika non-dualism stands in structural polemic with the dualist Saiddhāntika position consolidated by Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha II in Kashmir at the same time.

Abhinavagupta's Mālinī-Vijaya-Vārttika is the principal verse-commentary on the Mālinī-Vijaya-Tantra and the document that establishes the tantra as the scriptural ground of Trika; the Tantrāloka — the Trika summa — is structured throughout as an exposition of the Mālinī-Vijaya, citing it more often than any other source. The relation is engaged-from-within: Abhinavagupta is not refuting the tantra but doing its philosophy.

Abhinavagupta's articulation of cit as svaprakāśa and as pūrṇāhantā draws structurally on the broader northern-Indian doctrinal milieu within which the Maitreya-corpus's prakṛti-prabhāsvara-citta is operative. The non-substantialist phrasing of Buddha-nature in the Ratna-Gotra-Vibhāga (the garbha is not a substance but a pure potential for awakening realised through removal of adventitious defilements) parallels the Trika's account of mala as adventitious obscuration on the always-already-Śiva nature of awareness.

Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka and Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Vimarśinī engage Buddhist positions throughout; the Laṅkāvatāra-Sūtra's articulation of citta-mātra and of the distinction between the conditioned and unconditioned sides of consciousness is part of the doctrinal background against which the Trika non-dualist position is articulated, particularly in the treatment of vimarśa as the dynamic luminosity of cit.

core_thesis: Non-Vedāntin comparator (non-dual Śaiva, Trika/Pratyabhijñā). Abhinavagupta names his project, in *Tantrāloka* 1.16 and the surrounding *bhūmikā*, as *prakāśana* of the Trika *śāstra* in its full scope, drawing on three streams he treats as already lineage-internal: Utpaladeva's *Pratyabhijñā* analysis (cited throughout the *Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Vimarśinī*, esp. on *ĪPK* 1.1.1–5), the *Spanda* lineage (the dynamism of consciousness as pulse of manifestation), and the *Kula* / *Krama* tantric ritual systems (the architecture of *Tantrāloka* 1, esp. *TĀ* 1.1–50 establishing *Anuttara*-metaphysics). The single reality is *Anuttara* — Śiva-as-consciousness — whose intrinsic *svātantrya* and *vimarśa* freely manifest the entire continuum of *tattvas* by *ābhāsa* (*TĀ* 3.1–67 on the seventeen *kalās* of the *Anuttara*-pulse; *Parātrīśikā-Vivaraṇa* on *PT* 1–2 on *anuttara* as the *A*-phoneme); *spanda* names this unbroken pulsation by which manifestation as a whole occurs, closer in scope to *prakṛti* or *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa* (consistent with *Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1 *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ*; thematised at *TĀ* 4.181ff.). The bound subject's apparent limitation is *aṇavamala*, *māyīyamala*, *kārmamala* — three contractions of *pūrṇāhantā*, all themselves modes of Śiva's own *svātantrya* (*TĀ* 9.85–117 on the three *malas*). Soteriology is graded across *upāyas* — *āṇava* (*TĀ* 5), *śākta* (*TĀ* 4), *śāmbhava* (*TĀ* 3), *anupāya* (*TĀ* 2) — culminating in immediate *pratyabhijñā*. Aesthetic experience (*rasa*) is for him a window onto this same self-recognition: the *camatkāra* of poetic emotion is structurally identical with the *camatkāra* of liberation (*Abhinava-Bhāratī* on *Nāṭya-Śāstra* 6.31–33; *Dhvanyāloka-Locana* 1.1). Compared to Vedānta this is a positive non-dualism in which manifestation is real as Śiva's free expression, not as superimposition on a featureless absolute (the contrast Abhinavagupta thematises against the *brahmavāda* opponent at *ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 2.3.17).

engaged_works.title: Tantraloka
engaged_works.summary: The encyclopedic Trika compendium in 37 *āhnikas*, with Jayaratha's massive *Viveka* commentary. The first *āhnika* establishes the foundational metaphysics: a single *Anuttara*-consciousness whose *svātantrya* manifests the universe by *ābhāsa* (*TĀ* 1.1–50; the fifteen-fold typology of recognition at *TĀ* 1.142–151); the soteriological architecture is then graded across *anupāya* (*TĀ* 2.1–50), *śāmbhava* (*TĀ* 3.1–294), *śākta* (*TĀ* 4.1–278), and *āṇava-upāya* (*TĀ* 5.1–158). Subsequent sections systematize the *tattva*-hierarchy (*TĀ* 6 and 8–11), the analysis of the *malas* as contractions of *pūrṇāhantā* (*TĀ* 9.85–117), the doctrine of the *cakras* and the *mantra-vīrya* (*TĀ* 5.131–158, *TĀ* 12), the *Krama*-sequence of cognition's self-display (*TĀ* 4.118–181 on the twelve Kālīs), and the full ritual apparatus — *dīkṣā* (*TĀ* 15–23), *yāga* (*TĀ* 26–28), *homa* (*TĀ* 29) — read as enacted recognitions. The *Tantrāloka* is the locus classicus for Trika's claim that ritual, philosophy, aesthetics, and yoga are convergent paths to the same recognition (the programmatic statement at *TĀ* 1.149–150). For comparative work it provides the most fully-articulated alternative to Vedāntic non-dualism: the universe as Śiva's freely-projected real manifestation, not as superimposition on a witnessing absolute (*TĀ* 4.118 on *spanda* as manifestation's pulse).

engaged_works.title: Tantrasara
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed after the Tantrāloka as its prose-summary digest.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored prose digest of the Tantrāloka.
engaged_works.summary: Abhinavagupta's prose-mode digest of the *Tantrāloka*, organized in twenty-two *āhnikas* mirroring the structure of the larger work but omitting most of the elaborate ritual-procedural detail in favor of the philosophical and soteriological core. The *Tantrasāra* is the standard pedagogical entry into the *Tantrāloka*'s system: it establishes the metaphysics of *Anuttara*-consciousness and *svātantrya* (*Tantrasāra* 1, condensing *TĀ* 1); gives compact statements of the *upāya*-hierarchy — *anupāya* (*Tantrasāra* 2), *śāmbhava* (*Tantrasāra* 3), *śākta* (*Tantrasāra* 4), *āṇava* (*Tantrasāra* 5); explains the doctrine of the *malas* as *pūrṇāhantā*-contractions (*Tantrasāra* 9, condensing *TĀ* 9.85ff.); and presents the *Krama*-sequence (*Tantrasāra* 4, condensing *TĀ* 4.118ff.) and *mantra-vīrya* doctrines in summary form. Where the *Tantrāloka* takes thirty-seven *āhnikas* and the entirety of Jayaratha's *Viveka* to be navigable, the *Tantrasāra* can be read in a single sitting and is therefore the canonical textbook for the school's living transmission. Doctrinally identical to the *Tantrāloka*, it is the *Tantrāloka*'s own author's recommendation for the practitioner who needs the system without the encyclopedic apparatus (*Tantrasāra* preamble).

engaged_works.title: Malini-Vijaya-Varttika
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in the same period as the early Tantrāloka.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-commentary on the Mālinī-Vijaya-Tantra.
engaged_works.summary: Verse-*vārttika* on the *Mālinī-Vijaya-Tantra* — the scriptural source-text Abhinavagupta repeatedly identifies as the master tantra of the Trika system (cf. *TĀ* 1.17–18; *MVV* 1.1–25 on the tantra's authority) and on which the *Tantrāloka* is itself substantially built. The extant *Vārttika* covers principally the first *adhikāra* of the tantra (the work was likely never completed for the entire scripture) but in that range it gives Abhinavagupta's most direct exposition of the relation between Trika philosophical doctrine and tantric scripture: the metaphysical theses of *Anuttara* (*MVV* 1.108–172 on *anuttara* as the *A*-phoneme and as Śiva-as-*pūrṇāhantā*), *svātantrya*, the *tattva*-hierarchy, the structure of the *malas*, and the soteriological topography of the *upāyas* are shown to be already present, in compressed and ritually-coded form, in the *Mālinī-Vijaya* itself. The *Vārttika* is therefore the school's primary document on Trika hermeneutics: how revealed tantra is to be read as already philosophical (*MVV* 1.629ff. on the integration of scripture and analysis). The text is also a rich source on the *mātṛkā* (phonemic energies; *MVV* 1.150–250) and on the *Mālinī*-arrangement of the Sanskrit phonemes that gives the tantra its name.

engaged_works.title: Ishvara-Pratyabhijna-Vimarshini
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Utpaladeva's Kārikā.
engaged_works.summary: The shorter (*laghvī*) of Abhinavagupta's two commentaries on Utpaladeva's *Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā*; a precise philosophical exposition of the *Pratyabhijñā* system in dialectical engagement with Buddhist epistemology (especially Dharmakīrti, named at *ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 1.2.1ff.). The *Vimarśinī* sharpens the *prakāśa-vimarśa* analysis of consciousness (*ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.10–17, the canonical *prakāśa-vimarśa* discussion), defends the necessity of a unitary self-aware subject against Yogācāra/Sautrāntika atomism of cognitive moments (*ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 1.3.1–7 on memory as requiring a persistent recognizer), articulates *ābhāsa-vāda* as a positive theory in which manifestation is the supreme consciousness's own self-display (*ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.18–21 and *ĪPK* 2.3.1–17), and develops the *aham*-analysis — the *pūrṇāhantā* in which all objects are contained as the supreme *I*'s own *vimarśa*-content (*ĪPV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.13–14, *ĪPK* 4.1). The text thereby establishes Pratyabhijñā as a fully articulated philosophical school capable of debating epistemic Buddhists on their own ground. For comparative purposes it preserves the most rigorous defense of consciousness-as-self-aware-agent within the Indic tradition.

engaged_works.title: Ishvara-Pratyabhijna-Vivrti-Vimarshini
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed after the shorter Vimarśinī; Abhinavagupta's mature philosophical work.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the longer (*bṛhatī*) of the two Vimarśinīs on Utpaladeva's Kārikā.
engaged_works.summary: The longer (*bṛhatī*) of Abhinavagupta's two commentaries on the *Īśvara-Pratyabhijñā-Kārikā* — a sub-commentary that takes as its base not Utpaladeva's own short *Vṛtti* (as the shorter *Vimarśinī* does) but his long *Vivṛti* / *Ṭīkā* (now mostly lost, surviving largely through citation in this very text). The *Vivṛti-Vimarśinī* is the most extensive and philosophically demanding work of the entire Pratyabhijñā corpus: it elaborates in massive detail the *prakāśa-vimarśa* analysis of consciousness (*ĪPVV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.10–17), the *ābhāsa-vāda* (*ĪPVV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.18–21 and *ĪPK* 2.3), the *aham*-as-*pūrṇāhantā* doctrine (*ĪPVV* on *ĪPK* 1.5.13–14 and *ĪPK* 4.1), and the school's engagement with Buddhist epistemology — especially Dharmakīrti and Dharmottara (*ĪPVV* on *ĪPK* 1.2.1–11 and *ĪPK* 1.4) — and with Nyāya (*ĪPVV* on *ĪPK* 2.2.1–7). It is also the principal source for reconstructing the lost *Vivṛti* and therefore for understanding the full historical development of the Pratyabhijñā argumentation. The text is the school's most rigorous philosophical statement and the standard reference for any technical engagement with the *Pratyabhijñā* on its own analytic ground.

engaged_works.title: Dhvanyaloka-Locana
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: An early-to-middle period work; companion to the Abhinava-Bhāratī in Abhinavagupta's aesthetic corpus.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Ānandavardhana's Dhvanyāloka.
engaged_works.summary: Commentary (*Locana*, "Eye") on Ānandavardhana's *Dhvanyāloka* — the foundational treatise of *dhvani*-theory, the doctrine that the soul of poetry is suggestion (*vyañjanā*) rather than direct denotation (*abhidhā*) or secondary indication (*lakṣaṇā*) (*Dhvanyāloka* 1.1 and *Locana* ad loc.). Abhinavagupta's *Locana* both defends Ānandavardhana's position against the then-influential anti-*dhvani* schools — Mukula Bhaṭṭa, the Mīmāṃsaka reductionists (*Locana* on *Dhvanyāloka* 1.4–13) — and develops *dhvani*-theory toward integration with *rasa*-theory: the deepest *dhvani*-effect is *rasa-dhvani*, the suggestion of *rasa* (*Locana* on *Dhvanyāloka* 2.1–4), and the appreciation of suggested *rasa* in the cultivated *sahṛdaya* is the structural counterpart to the soteriological *camatkāra* developed in the *Abhinava-Bhāratī*. The *Locana* is thus the bridge text between *dhvani* poetics and the Trika-grounded aesthetics of *rasa* — the *sthāyi-bhāva*'s *sādhāraṇī-karaṇa* (universalization) yielding aesthetic delight is, on Abhinavagupta's reading, a transparency of consciousness to its own intrinsic *ānanda* (*Locana* on *Dhvanyāloka* 2.4 on the *ānanda*-character of *rasa*-experience), the same recognition the *Pratyabhijñā* programme aims at by other means. Together with the *Abhinava-Bhāratī* it grounds all subsequent Sanskrit literary theory.

engaged_works.title: Abhinava-Bharati on Natya-Shastra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on Bharata's Nāṭya-Śāstra; the locus classicus of rasa-theology in Trika idiom.
engaged_works.summary: The definitive commentary on Bharata's *Nāṭya-Śāstra* and the locus classicus of *rasa*-theory in its mature form. Abhinavagupta reviews and refutes the prior *rasa-sūtra* readings — Bhaṭṭa Lollaṭa, Śaṅkuka, Bhaṭṭa Nāyaka, all examined at *Abhinava-Bhāratī* on *Nāṭya-Śāstra* 6.31 (the *rasa-sūtra*) — and establishes his own *siddhānta* of *rasa* as a generalized (*sādhāraṇī-kṛta*) aesthetic apprehension in which the spectator's ordinary egoic *citta-vṛtti*s are momentarily suspended and the *sthāyi-bhāva* manifests in the consciousness of the cultivated *sahṛdaya* as savoured *camatkāra* (*Abhinava-Bhāratī* on *Nāṭya-Śāstra* 6.32–33). The deep claim — Trika in everything but vocabulary — is that aesthetic experience is structurally identical with the soteriological recognition: in both, the limited subject's contractions briefly drop and the *pūrṇāhantā* becomes self-luminous as wonder (*Abhinava-Bhāratī* on *NŚ* 6.31, the locus that grounds the *camatkāra–pratyabhijñā* identification). *Rasa* is thus not a property of the artwork but a transparency of consciousness to its own intrinsic delight (*ānanda*), making aesthetics a philosophical discipline continuous with *Pratyabhijñā* metaphysics. The text grounds all subsequent Sanskrit aesthetic theory.

engaged_works.title: Paratrishika-Vivarana
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary on the Parātrīśikā tantra.
engaged_works.summary: Extended commentary on the *Parātrīśikā* — a short tantric text appended to the *Rudra-Yāmala* — that Abhinavagupta uses as the occasion for one of his deepest expositions of *Anuttara* metaphysics. The *Vivaraṇa* unfolds the doctrine of the supreme *parā-vāc* (transcendent speech) as the subtle pulse (*spanda*) of consciousness from which the manifest universe emerges through *paśyantī* and *madhyamā* into *vaikharī* (*Parātrīśikā-Vivaraṇa* on *PT* 5–9, on the four-fold *vāc*); the entire tattva-hierarchy is read as the self-articulation of the supreme *aham* through its own *mātṛkā* (the phonemic energies; *PTV* on *PT* 5–9). The work develops in detail the *Anuttara*-as-*A* analysis — the first phoneme as the seed of the entire emanation (*PTV* on *PT* 1–2, the foundational *anuttara*-section) — the structure of *kula* and the *trika* of Parā–Parāparā–Aparā (*PTV* on *PT* 3–4), and the soteriological significance of recognizing one's own consciousness as already *parā-vāc* (*PTV* on *PT* 9–10 and concluding section). Among Abhinavagupta's writings this is the most concentrated text on the *spanda*/manifestation interface — directly relevant to comparative work on consciousness-as-engine-of-manifestation.
